feature_name: C64x+ VLIW core and instruction set
description: The device integrates a TMS320C64x+ fixed-point DSP core with an eight-issue VLIW pipeline dispatching multiple operations per cycle. It supports software pipelining, saturation arithmetic, wide bit manipulation, and SIMD-style 16-bit lane operations. Harvard program/data paths sustain throughput under real-time constraints. L1 caches plus configurable L2 reduce external memory stalls when tuned. Deterministic timing depends on schedule discipline, cache planning, and toolchain conventions.
evidence: CPU/ISA reference (SPRU732): pipeline/ISA chapters
dependencies: None; optional cache and memory configuration during init
Remarks: Organize hot loops for software pipelining; validate with deterministic microbenchmarks; avoid pipeline side effects in ISR paths.

description: An on-chip L1P cache reduces instruction fetch latency for tight loops and hot paths. Typical capacity ~32 KB with line fill/eviction under core control and prefetch effects. It is transparent but benefits from code locality and alignment. L2 can stage critical code as SRAM to improve determinism. Cold-start penalties should be budgeted for latency-sensitive routines.
evidence: SPRU732: L1P cache chapter
dependencies: Optional cache configuration via startup code
Remarks: Keep time-critical code contiguous; consider locking in L2 SRAM; pre-warm critical paths before deadlines.

description: The L1D cache accelerates frequent data access and reduces load/store stall cycles. Typical size ~32 KB closely coupled to L/S units for single-cycle hits. DMA and MMIO must be coordinated to avoid coherency hazards with write-back policies. L2 configuration complements L1D for predictable latency on larger working sets. Buffer placement/alignment strongly influence effective bandwidth.
evidence: SPRU732: L1D cache chapter
dependencies: Cache maintenance or non-cacheable regions when using DMA
Remarks: Align structures to cache lines; writeback/invalidate around DMA buffers; map MMIO non-cacheable.

description: L2 can be partitioned as SRAM, cache, or a mix to balance capacity and determinism. Locking critical code/data in L2 SRAM prevents eviction-driven jitter. As cache, L2 extends the hot working set at lower latency than external DDR. Early, stable partitioning avoids integration drift. Proper L2 tuning underpins predictable real-time behavior.
evidence: SPRU732: L2/Memory map
dependencies: Startup configuration of L2 and memory attributes
Remarks: Reserve L2 SRAM for ISRs, stacks, descriptors; document partition and enforce in builds.

description: IDMA performs fast internal copies between L2 and L1 with minimal setup latency. It is CPU-triggered and best for short, deterministic staging of code/data. Simpler than EDMA but restricted to on-chip spaces. Complements EDMA which handles bulk or peripheral-driven transfers. Cache coordination ensures destination visibility.
evidence: SPRU732: IDMA registers (SRC/DST/COUNT/STAT)
dependencies: None; coordinate with cache settings
Remarks: Use IDMA for short deterministic copies; prefer EDMA for large/chained moves; perform cache maintenance where required.

description: The DDR2 controller initializes and services external SDRAM per JEDEC requirements. Software must program timing (tRFC/tRP/tRCD/etc.) and issue power-up/mode register sequences precisely. Refresh (SDRCR) and drive/VTP settings must match the board. Post-init pattern tests validate timing margins. Enable caches/DMA only after integrity is proven.
evidence: DDR2: SDCR, SDTIMR, SDTIMR2, SDRCR (device DDR chapter)
dependencies: PSC enable; stable PLL/clock; board-specific DDR parameters
Remarks: Derive timings from the DRAM datasheet; verify with stress tests; fence high-throughput clients until DDR is validated.

description: EDMA3 moves data between memory and peripherals using PaRAM sets with link/chain features. Channels route to queues and transfer controllers with arbitration. Completion and error paths use IPR/ICR and CCERR. QDMA supports fast CPU-initiated copies. Descriptor alignment and coherency are critical for correctness and performance.
evidence: EDMA3: DCHMAP/QCHMAP, DMAQNUM, PaRAM (OPT,SRC,A_B_CNT,DST,BIDX,CIDX,LINK,BCNTRLD), EER/EESR, IPR/ICR, CCERR
dependencies: PSC enable for CC/TC; INTC mapping; optional event routing
Remarks: Place PaRAM/descriptors in coherent memory; clear IPR via ICR; size bursts to bus/cache widths; isolate error ISR paths.

description: The EMAC provides 10/100/1000-Mbps Ethernet with MAC control and statistics. Packet I/O uses descriptor-based DMA rings; MDIO manages external PHY link and configuration. Proper bring-up configures MACCONTROL, address filters, and enables RX/TX paths. PHY discovery/config via MDIO must precede traffic enable. Cache coherency and alignment are required for descriptor rings.
evidence: EMAC: MACCONTROL, MACADDRHI/LO, STATs; MDIO: MDIOCONTROL, MDIOUSERACCESS0/1
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; MDIO clock; PHYs; DMA rings; INTC
Remarks: Initialize MDIO and confirm link; program MACCONTROL and unicast filters; ensure rings/buffers are coherent and aligned.

description: The PCI peripheral supports conventional PCI host/slave operation for high-throughput host links. Software programs the configuration header, BAR0–BAR5, and COMMAND/STATUS to expose internal windows. Interrupts and EDMA assist bulk transfers. Reset sequencing and host negotiation are required at bring-up. Endianness and address translation must match the host view.
evidence: PCI: CONFIG_HEADER (COMMAND/STATUS, CLASSREV), BAR0–BAR5, INTx; device PCI chapter
dependencies: PSC enable; host reset; BAR/window map; EDMA for bulk
Remarks: Negotiate reset with host; validate BAR mappings; confirm endian/alignment; prefer EDMA for sustained movement.

description: UHPI allows an external host to access DSP memory-mapped resources directly. Control covers bus width, reset, and ready/hold handshakes. It supports polled or interrupt-driven operation. Useful for host boot and diagnostics. Arbitration must avoid contention with core/DMA masters.
evidence: UHPI: HPIC, HPIA, HPID (peripheral chapter)
dependencies: PSC enable; host timing/handshake; INTC if used
Remarks: Program HPIC for width/reset; validate handshake timing; partition shared regions to avoid races.

description: VLYNQ provides a point-to-point serial link mapping a peer’s resources into local space. Configuration sets lane rate, framing, and address windows. Error/interrupt status assists robust operation. Both ends must match configuration for link bring-up. Small transactions should validate windows before enabling DMA over the link.
evidence: VLYNQ: control/status and address window mapping regs (device VLYNQ chapter)
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; peer configuration; window mapping
Remarks: Coordinate parameters with the peer; verify mappings with test reads/writes; stage to DMA only after sanity tests.

description: McBSP supports multichannel TDM/I2S-like audio and telecom interfaces. Independent RX/TX sections use DRR/DXR FIFOs with rich interrupts. Sample rate is generated via SRGR; frame formats by RCR/XCR. Pin control via PCR selects clock/frame polarities. EDMA typically services RX/TX to minimize CPU overhead.
evidence: McBSP: SPCR, RCR, XCR, SRGR, PCR, DRR, DXR
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; EDMA setup; INTC mapping
Remarks: Program SRGR and frame control before enabling SPCR; confirm word/slot timing; size EDMA bursts to frame boundaries.

description: The SPI module supports master/slave transfers with programmable CPOL/CPHA and word length. Inter-word delays and chip-select polarity/timing are configurable. Data moves via SPIDATx/SPIBUF with optional interrupts. EDMA can offload long transfers. Correct electrical CS routing is required for multi-slave systems.
evidence: SPI: SPIGCR0/1, SPIFMT0–3, SPIDELAY, SPIBUF, SPIDAT0/1
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; CS wiring; optional EDMA/interrupts
Remarks: Reset/configure SPIGCR0/1; select CPOL/CPHA to match slave; align buffers if using EDMA.

description: I2C supports standard/fast modes with programmable SCL timing. ICMDR controls transfers; ICSTR reports status/errors. External pull-ups on SDA/SCL are mandatory. Interrupts cover ready, NACK, arbitration loss, bus busy, and completion. Multi-byte frames require correct ICCNT and START/STOP ordering.
evidence: I2C: ICOAR, ICSAR, ICPSC, ICCLKL/ICCLKH, ICCNT, ICMDR, ICSTR, ICIER
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; external pull-ups; INTC mapping
Remarks: Handle NACK/arbitration lost per ICSTR; program ICCNT; assert STOP only after ACK of final byte.

description: 16550-like UART with configurable data bits, parity, and stop bits. RX/TX FIFOs reduce interrupt load at higher baud rates. Baud derives from DLL/DLH divisors with optional prescaling. Module clocking/reset uses PWREMU_MGMT. EDMA or interrupts can service data depending on throughput targets.
evidence: UART: THR/RBR, LCR, FCR, DLL/DLH, IER/IIR, PWREMU_MGMT
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; reference clock; INTC mapping
Remarks: Reset FIFOs; compute divisors and verify baud error; choose ISR vs EDMA based on sustained rate.

description: Up to 32 GPIOs configurable as inputs/outputs with per-bit direction. Data writes via SET_DATA/CLR_DATA; reads via IN_DATA. Edge detection supports rising/falling with latched status. Bank-level enables and INTSTAT route interrupts to the controller. Some banks can source EDMA sync events.
evidence: GPIO: DIR, SET_DATA, CLR_DATA, IN_DATA, SET_RIS_TRIG, CLR_RIS_TRIG, BINTEN, INTSTAT01
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; INTC mapping; optional EDMA sync
Remarks: Enable via PSC; clear latched flags after ISR; add external debounce if required.

description: A 64-bit counter can split into two independent 32-bit timers. Period and control configure one-shot or periodic operation with compare events. Interrupts on compare/overflow drive ticks/timeouts. Capture/PWM functions depend on pinmux. Prescaler must be computed from the known input clock.
evidence: Timer: TIM12/TIM34, PRD12/PRD34, TCR, TGCR, INTCTL
dependencies: PSC enable; INTC mapping; optional PINMUX for capture/PWM
Remarks: Choose 64-bit vs dual 32-bit early; compute prescalers; keep ISR short and defer work.

description: The PLL multiplies a reference clock to generate CPU/system rates, with dividers producing peripheral clocks. Reprogramming requires the documented lock/unlock sequence using PLLCMD/PLLSTAT. All dependent divider changes must be applied coherently. Clocks must stabilize before enabling DDR and high-speed peripherals. Bring-up should measure derived clocks on test points.
evidence: PLLC: PLLCTL, PLLM, PLLDIV1–n, PLLCMD, PLLSTAT
dependencies: Stable ref clock; correct programming order; PSC sequencing
Remarks: Follow the lock sequence; poll PLLSTAT; update dividers atomically; verify outputs with instrumentation.

description: PSC controls power/clock gating of modules by domains. Each LPSC must be transitioned to ENABLE before register access. Transitions are requested via PTCMD and observed with PTSTAT/MDSTAT. Proper sequencing prevents bus hangs and saves energy. Unused modules should be gated in steady state.
evidence: PSC: PTCMD, PTSTAT, MDCTL[n], MDSTAT[n]
dependencies: Required by all peripheral drivers; domain ordering
Remarks: Enable before access; poll for completion; disable unused modules to reduce power.

description: System PINMUX selects peripheral functions on multiplexed pins; protected writes require KICK unlock. Correct mux avoids signal conflicts and ensures availability. Boot straps may set initial mux that software must refine. Preserve reserved fields when updating PINMUX to prevent side effects.
evidence: System config: KICK0/KICK1 unlock keys; PINMUXn registers
dependencies: KICK unlock sequence; ordered with PSC to avoid unsafe toggles
Remarks: Perform early PINMUX setup; maintain a reviewed board mux table; preserve reserved bits on writes.

description: System events map to CPU interrupts via the event combiner and INTMUX. Proper ISR design clears peripheral status before acknowledging the controller to avoid retrigger. Vector table placement and cache behavior affect latency. Event masks and flags (EVTMASK/EVTFLAG/EVTCLR) control aggregation. Centralized setup simplifies priority and routing.
evidence: INTC: INTMUX1–n; Event combiner: EVTMASK, EVTFLAG, EVTCLR
dependencies: Peripheral enables; INTMUX mapping; optional event combiner
Remarks: Minimize ISR prolog/epilog; clear peripheral flags first; defer noncritical work.

description: Boot sources include I2C, SPI, UART, PCI, or HPI selected by strap pins and ROM. The ROM performs minimal init then loads a first-stage image to internal memory. Application code completes PLL/PSC/DDR setup. Capabilities can vary by ROM/silicon revision, which must be noted during bring-up.
evidence: Reset/Boot chapter: boot pins and modes
dependencies: Board straps; ROM revision; boot media present
Remarks: Keep first-stage minimal; hand off to unified board init that programs PLL/PSC/DDR deterministically.

description: The IDLE instruction halts the core clock until an enabled interrupt occurs. PSC can further gate clocks/domains for deeper savings. Wake sources must remain active and routed correctly. Software must coordinate entry/exit to maintain data integrity and timing. Use instead of busy polling when latency budgets permit.
evidence: SPRU732: IDLE; PSC domain gating
dependencies: Wake interrupt routing; PSC domain config
Remarks: Use IDLE in wait loops; ensure wake interrupts are unmasked; quiesce DMA before gating.

description: A JTAG interface supports real-time debug, trace, and halt. Emulation-aware controls keep selected clocks active while halted. Debug can perturb timers/DMA/links, so sessions must consider protocol timeouts. Attach order and boot timing influence behavior. Follow emulator guidance to avoid real-time interference.
evidence: SPRU732: Emulation; device EMU notes
dependencies: External emulator; toolchain integration
Remarks: Avoid halting during active transfers; enable emulation-aware options; script attach sequences.

description: Peripherals expose sticky status/error flags, typically write-1-to-clear. EDMA reports completion/errors via IPR/ICR and CCERR and must be serviced promptly. Centralized error paths and logging simplify triage. Counters/timestamps help identify recurring faults. Distinguish expected transients from fatal errors in policy.
evidence: EDMA: IPR/ICR/CCERR; peripheral status/clear regs
dependencies: Per-driver ISR/polling; logging infra
Remarks: Unify error-reporting; follow W1C semantics; capture diagnostics for field issues.

description: MAR registers define cacheability and allocation policy for external regions. Proper setup ensures DMA buffers and MMIO windows behave as intended. These settings complement L2 partitioning for balanced performance/determinism. Misconfiguration can cause stale data or bus faults under load. Initialize early and validate with cacheability tests.
evidence: SPRU732: MAR0–MAR63 (cacheability)
dependencies: Privileged CPU setup; coordinated with cache/DMA
Remarks: Mark MMIO non-cacheable; isolate DMA buffers to coherent regions; document MAR policy.

description: UNKNOWN. Public collateral for this device does not document dedicated security engines or secure boot. If present, features are not described in the standard manuals referenced. Software should not assume hardware crypto/secure boot availability. Reassess if newer silicon or platform documentation indicates otherwise.
evidence: UNKNOWN
dependencies: UNKNOWN
Remarks: UNKNOWN
